core_takeaway: The first position looks strongly advantaged. Agent_1 opened by claiming Jewel and Pencil, the two highest-value items under identical preferences, and the rest of the negotiation treated those items as protected anchors. Agents 2 and 3 fought over the much smaller remaining pool, so the last position improved from zero-risk outcomes to Quill+Stone but never threatened Agent_1's top-two bundle.
why_or_counterexample: Yes. The outcome was not just a fair split of differentiated tastes: all agents had the same values, and Agent_1 ended with the two most valuable items. The counterweight is that the last agent's veto power mattered enough to avoid zero allocations and secure Quill+Stone, but that only softened the loss; it did not dislodge the first speaker's anchor.

phase_notes.public_discussion: Agent_1's first public message proposed keeping Jewel+Pencil, and both later agents quickly accepted that premise. Public debate then narrowed to Apple, Quill, and Stone, with Options B, C, and Pivot A all preserving Agent_1's top-two bundle.
phase_notes.private_thinking: Private thinking repeatedly named Jewel and Pencil as the hard stability anchors. Even Agents 2 and 3 planned around those anchors rather than challenging them, focusing on how to rebalance the leftover items.
phase_notes.formal_proposals: Every formal proposal in all three rounds assigned Jewel and Pencil to Agent_1. Early proposals failed because they mishandled the leftovers or left Agent_3 empty; final convergence came only when all three proposed the same Option C split.
phase_notes.private_voting: Raw votes show Agent_2 privately resisting Apple-only deals when a higher payoff was available, and Agent_3 rejecting zero-payoff allocations. By round 3, all private votes accepted the identical anchor-preserving allocation.
phase_notes.reflection: Reflections consistently framed consensus as preserving Agent_1's top-two and managing the Agent_2 versus Agent_3 conflict over Apple, Quill, and Stone. The recommended path never reopened Jewel or Pencil.

mechanisms.theme: Opening anchor captured the top two items
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1 used the first speaking slot to define Jewel and Pencil as its bundle. Because these were the two highest-value items, that opening anchor set up a large payoff gap before the others responded.

evidence.quote: Agent_1 keeps Jewel and Pencil
it guarantees I hold my top two items
Jewel and Pencil are non-negotiable anchors

mechanisms.theme: Later agents accepted the protected-anchor frame
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_2 and Agent_3 did not seriously contest Jewel or Pencil. They publicly accepted the premise that Agent_1 would keep them, moving their own bargaining to the smaller items.

evidence.quote: I agree that Jewel and Pencil are your top priority
I accept the premise of keeping Jewel and Pencil with Agent_1.
I agree Agent_1 keeps Jewel + Pencil

mechanisms.theme: Identical preferences made the anchor especially valuable
mechanisms.explanation: The agents had the same item values, so the first-position edge is not explained by Agent_1 valuing different items. Agent_1 got Jewel+Pencil while Agent_3 got only Stone+Quill, creating the large first-minus-last gap.

mechanisms.theme: Conflict was redirected to the leftover pool
mechanisms.explanation: Once Agent_1's top-two claim was accepted, the negotiation energy centered on Apple, Quill, and Stone. That kept the first agent's high-value bundle insulated while Agents 2 and 3 traded off much smaller payoffs.

evidence.quote: The remaining contention centers on Quill
Jewel+Pencil with Agent_1; Apple with Agent_2; Quill+Stone with Agent_3
how to balance Quill (and Stone) between Agent_2 and Agent_3

mechanisms.theme: Private reasoning reinforced the first anchor
mechanisms.explanation: The raw private thinking shows the agents internalized the same constraint: Jewel and Pencil should stay with Agent_1. This private alignment explains why later public offers continued to protect the first position.

evidence.quote: Jewel and Pencil are the hard anchors for Agent_1 and should remain with me
Agent_1 is adamant about preserving Jewel (top priority) and Pencil
Agent_1 is protecting Jewel and Pencil as their top priorities

mechanisms.theme: Last-position veto softened but did not erase the disadvantage
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_3 was not powerless: zero-payoff proposals failed, and the final deal gave Agent_3 Quill+Stone. But this counterpressure operated only within the leftover pool and left Agent_1's Jewel+Pencil untouched.

evidence.quote: Agent_3 receives no items (0 utility)
Proposal 1 secures Quill and Stone for Agent_3
top-two preserved

mechanisms.theme: Final consensus locked in the first-position bundle
mechanisms.explanation: By the final round, all formal proposals were identical and kept Jewel+Pencil with Agent_1. The unanimous vote therefore finalized the first agent's original protected bundle rather than reopening it.

evidence.quote: Agent_1: 1:Jewel, 4:Pencil
Proposal #1: 3 accept, 0 reject
CONSENSUS REACHED! Proposal #3 accepted unanimously!
